coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of gold nanoparticles coated on flower-like MoS2 microsphere and their application for electrochemical nitrite sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Sai
Tang, Yuping
Chen, Yanyan
Zheng, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, 3D flower-like MoS2 microspheres with nanosheets were synthesized by a simple thiourea-assisted hydrothermal route and employed to support gold nanoparticles in order to fabricate a novel nitrite sensor. By this method, stable and well-distributed gold nanoparticles were formed due to the electrostatic attraction between APTES-functionalized MoS2 and citric-stabilized gold nanoparticles. Electrochemical investigations indicated that Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites exhibited an excellent performance towards nitrite oxidation. Under the optimal experimental condition (pH6.0), the sensor displayed a wide linear range from 5.0μM to 27.8mM with a high sensitivity of 117.0μAmM−1 cm−2 and a low detection limit of 1.67μM. In a word, the present study demonstrates that the Au NPs/MoS2 composites are promising electroactive materials for developing nitrite sensors with great potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2 microsphere
Gold nanoparticles
Nitrite sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM and TEM images of (A, C) MoS2 and (B, D) Au NPs/MoS2, respectively.
XRD pattern of prepared (a) MoS2 and (b) Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites.

(A) CVs of the (a) bare GCE, (b) MoS2/GCE and (c) Au NPs/MoS2/GCE in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (PH 6.0) in the presence of 4.0mM NO2 − at a scan rate of 100mVs−1. (B) amperometric response of the (a) bare GCE, (b) MoS2/GCE and (c) Au NPs/MoS2/GCE in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (PH 6.0) in the presence of 4.0mM NO2 −.

(A) CVs of the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (pH6.0) in the absence and presence of nitrite with different concentrations (from a to g: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 8mM at the scan rate of 100mVs−1). (B) The linear fitting program of the oxidation peak currents with the nitrite concentrations. (C) CVs of the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (pH6.0) containing 8mM nitrite at different scan rates (from a to g: 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180 and 200mVs−1). (D) The linear fitting program of the reduction peak currents with the square root of scan rate.

(A) Typical amperometric response of the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE on successive injection of nitrite into the stirring N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (pH6.0), applied potential: 0.8V. (B) Calibration curve of nitrite versus its concentration.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites and Au NPs/MoS2/GCE.
Comparison of proposed sensor for determination of NO2 − with others.
Detection of NO2 − in tap water samples by using Au NPs/MoS2/GCE.

row.entry: Total amount of NO2 − found a (μM)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation for five determinations by the sensor.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of gold nanoparticles coated on flower-like MoS2 microsphere and their application for electrochemical nitrite sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Shaanxi Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine for TCM Compatibility, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xi'an 712046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Shaanxi Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine for TCM Compatibility

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chinese Medicine Fundamentals and New Drugs Research, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xi'an 712046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chinese Medicine Fundamentals and New Drugs Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Collaborative Innovation Center of Chinese Medicinal Resources Industrialization, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xi'an 712046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Collaborative Innovation Center of Chinese Medicinal Resources Industrialization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, 3D flower-like MoS2 microspheres with nanosheets were synthesized by a simple thiourea-assisted hydrothermal route and employed to support gold nanoparticles in order to fabricate a novel nitrite sensor. By this method, stable and well-distributed gold nanoparticles were formed due to the electrostatic attraction between APTES-functionalized MoS2 and citric-stabilized gold nanoparticles. Electrochemical investigations indicated that Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites exhibited an excellent performance towards nitrite oxidation. Under the optimal experimental condition (pH6.0), the sensor displayed a wide linear range from 5.0μM to 27.8mM with a high sensitivity of 117.0μAmM−1 cm−2 and a low detection limit of 1.67μM. In a word, the present study demonstrates that the Au NPs/MoS2 composites are promising electroactive materials for developing nitrite sensors with great potential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite, a typical inorganic pollutant commonly used in various areas including food preservation, fertilizing industries and environmental systems [1–3]. A large amount of nitrite in the human body could readily reacts with hemoglobin to form methemoglobin and greatly decreases the oxygen transporting capacity of blood, resulting in serious hazards to human health and may cause cancer [4]. Besides, nitrite is always present in water distribution system owing to their use as fertilizers. The environmental problems caused by the contamination of water sources for human consumption are the subjects under investigations [5]. Hence, to develop an accurate, rapid and simple method for sensitive and selective determination of nitrite is very important in preventing their buildup to a potentially harmful concentration. Recently, diverse analytical techniques according to different standards, including chemiluminescence [6], chromatography [7], fluorimetry [8] and titrimetry [9] have been developed for nitrite determination. Among these methods, the electrochemical techniques have proven to be very promising alternatives because of their faster response, cheaper, and more advantageous real-time analysis [10–12].

Commonly, nitrite can be electrochemically determined by both oxidation and reduction, whereas oxidation is usually preferred because no interference of nitrate ion and molecular oxygen in anodic nitrite determination [13]. Nevertheless, the electrochemical oxidation of nitrite requires relatively higher potentials at the surfaces of bare electrode and decrease the sensitivity and accuracy. Thus, in order to reduce the overpotential and drastically minimized the fouling effect, it is necessary to exploit some suitable catalysts to modify the electrodes surface [14,15].

In recent years, an increasing number of metal nanoparticles (NPs) have been employed to modified sensors due to their large specific surface areas and unusual structural features. It is reported that electrodes modified with PdNPs [16], PtNPs [17], CuNPs [18] and AgNPs [19] have presented excellent catalytic activity for determining trace nitrite. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), as a typical noble metal catalyst, exhibit stronger catalytic activity for the oxidation of nitrite due to their highest conductivity and excellent catalytic properties. Many researchers have studied the electrochemical behavior of Au NPs towards nitrite oxidation. Losada et al. fabricated a nitrite sensor based on electrodeposited gold nanoparticles on ferrocenyl-dendrimer film [20]. Yadav et al. synthesized gold nanoparticles incorporated zinc based metal-organic framework to detect nitrite [21]. Zhuang et al. described a method for the fabrication of a nanohybrid composed of carbon dots (C-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by in-situ reduction of C-dots and hydroauric acid under alkaline conditions for nitrite sensing [22]. However, metal nanoparticles usually suffer from aggregation issues during their synthesis, which may severely prohibits extensive applications. Therefore, it is very important to highly dispersed metal nanoparticles in practical nitrite sensing applications.

Up to now, many semiconducting two-dimensional nanomaterial have received considerable interest and been identified as potential candidates for the fabrication of next generation sensing devices [23,24]. Layered molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), as a typical family member of transition-metal sulfides, have earned much attention due to its high surface-to-volume ratio, excellent nanoelectronics and unique semiconducting properties, which are indirect and direct band gap for bulk (1.29eV) and monolayer (1.9eV) caused by quantum confinement effect, respectively [25]. Similar to graphene, MoS2 has a layered structure, where each layer consists of covalently bonded MoS atoms and the neighboring layers attach each other by van der Waals forces [26]. In layered MoS2, the two-dimensional electron-electron correlations among Mo atoms enhanced planar electric transportation. Recent studies revealed that although pure MoS2 own the disadvantages like poor charge mobility and low activity [27], the defects and edges on MoS2 contain partially unbound sulfur, which can act as sites decorated for supporting noble metal nanoparticles [28], thus achieving amplify signals and increasing the performance of sensors [29,30]. In addition, MoS2 nanosheets could provide effective support matrix for noble nanoparticles dispersion and stabilization [31]. It is gratified that not only the MoS2 nanosheets made the noble metal nanoparticles more dispersed, but also the synergistic effect between MoS2 nanosheets and noble metal nanoparticles could further enhance the conductivity and catalytic performance distinctly than pure MoS2. Qiao et al. reported an effective and stable noble metal free MoS2 catalyst for the reduction of p-NP with NaBH4 under mild reaction conditions. The activity of MoS2NSs can be greatly enhanced after decorating the MoS2 surface with noble metal (Au, Ag, Pd, Pt) NPs [32]. Hou et al. prepared hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) electrodes composed of Pt nanoparticles and MoS2 nanosheets grown on carbon fibers. The fast electron transfer between the catalytic edge sites of MoS2 and Pt nanoparticles, and high electrochemical effective surface area of the electrode substrate are the key factors contributing to the superior HER kinetics [33]. These all demonstrate that MoS2 nanosheets and noble metal nanoparticles can provide a generic synthetic path way for the enhanced hybrid nanomaterials.

In our work, we present a facile preparation route to synthesize gold nanoparticles coated on the surface of flower-like MoS2 microspheres and the Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposite was used to fabricate a novel nitrite sensor. The morphology and structure of this nanocomposite were characterized, and its electrochemical properties were also investigated in details. Considering the excellent experimental results, it is possible for the idea to be widely applied in electrochemistry, catalysis and biological molecules determinations.

Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Acros Organics, respectively. Thiourea (UA) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (3-APTES) were gotten from Aladdin. Chitosan (CS, MW 5–6×105, >90% deacetylation) was purchased from Shanghai Reagent Company. All other reagents and chemicals were analytical reagent grade and aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water from a Millipore system (>18MΩ·cm).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were carried out by Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (FEI, USA). Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were carried out by JSM 6700F (JEOL, Japan). X ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were taken by D/MAX 3C (Rigaku, Japan). Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a conventional three-electrode electroanalysis system controlled by EC 550 electrochemical workstation (Gaoss Union Technology Co., Ltd., Wuhan, China) and CHI 660 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Co. Ltd., China). A conventional three-electrode cell was used, including a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, geometric area=0.07cm2) as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (3M KCl) electrode as the reference electrode and platinum foil as the counter electrode. All potentials given in this work were referred to the Ag/AgCl electrode and all electrochemical experiments were conducted at room temperature (25±2°C).

180mg Na2MoO4·2H2O and 350mg thiourea were added successively in 100mL deionized water and stirred with magnetic stirrer for 30min [34]. Next, the whole reaction mixture was transferred to a 100mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200°C for 24h. The black sediment was centrifuged, washed with copious amount of water and ethanol (each washing 3 times) successively and vacuum dried in oven at 60°C for overnight.

The citrate-capped AuNPs with negative valence were synthesized by a reported procedure [35]. Briefly, 1mL of HAuCl4 (1wt%) was added to 24mL of distilled water in a flask and then heated to 100°C in an oil bath with vigorous stirring. Then, 1mL of sodium citrate (50mg) was added to the above solution quickly. The last, the mixture react for 20min at 100°C. The resulting Au NPs colloid was cooled to room temperature through vigorous stirring.

The schematic illustration of the preparation of flower-like MoS2 and Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Briefly, the synthesized 60mg MoS2 was suspended in methanol (20mL), followed by adding 300μL of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) under stirring overnight. The function of APTES is to provide positive valence on MoS2 and produce the APTES-functionalized MoS2. Then, the solution was centrifuged, washed and dried in air. Finally, the resulting precipitate was dispersed in water and mixed together with the citrate-capped AuNPs collosol and stirred for 24h. After which the hybrid of flower-like MoS2 and Au nanoparticles were separated from the solution by centrifugation at 10000rpm for 3min, washed with ultrapure water and dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 12h.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was prepared by a simple casting method. Prior to use, GCE was polished with 0.3 and 1.0μm alumina powder to obtain mirror like surface, respectively, then rinsed with doubly distilled water, followed by sonication in ethanol solution and doubly distilled water successively. Then, the GCE was allowed to dry in a stream of nitrogen. 2.0mg of Au NPs/MoS2 were dispersed into chitosan (2mL, CS, MW 5–6×105, >90% deacetylation) and sonicated for 10min. The suspension (6μL) was dropped onto the pre-polished mirror like surface of GCE and then dried in air at room temperature.

Fig. 1(A) and (B) show the SEM images of MoS2 and Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites, respectively. From Fig. 1(A), an overall morphology of the MoS2 microsphere exhibits the nanoflower MoS2 consists of typical two dimensional sheets flower-like structure with the diameter about 500nm. Fig. 1(B) indicates that the gold nanoparticles uniformly deposited on the surface of flower-like MoS2 nanosheets. Meanwhile, the MoS2 and Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites were further characterized by TEM. In Fig. 1(C), it can be seen clearly that the specific flower-like MoS2 structure is formed by soft silk films with wrinkle which looks like the structure of monolayer graphene. As shown in Fig. 1(D), it can be found that flower-like MoS2 nanosheets provided a large surface area to decorate with numerous Au NPs, suggesting that the Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites were synthesized successfully.

The composition of MoS2, APTES-MoS2 and Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites were identified by EDS pattern. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the MoS2 was composed of Mo and S elements, the APTES-MoS2 was composed of C, N, Si, Mo and S elements, and the Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites were composed of Au, Mo and S elements. These results further indicated that the Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by this synthetic method.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of MoS2 nanoflowers and Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites. As seen from the pattern of MoS2 nanosheets (curve a), the detected three peaks with 2θ values of 11.1°, 32.27° and 58.23° that can be indexed as the (002), (100) and (110) planes, respectively. This results confirmed that the synthesized product was MoS2 with hexagonal structure, and all the diffraction peaks are in good agreement with the standard JCPDS data (JCPDS Card No. 37–1492) [36,37]. Curve b shows another four strong diffraction peaks at 2θ=37.1°, 44.3°, 64.6° and 77.6° can be assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystalline planes of the face-centered cubic form of gold (JCPDS Card No. 65–2870), respectively [38]. No peaks from other impurities are detected in the XRD pattern, indicating that the sample was highly crystalline.

According to the previous studies [39], the reaction of synthesis MoS2 consists of three steps: (a) the formation of H2S through the hydrolysis of thiourea; (b) the reduction of Mo (VI) to Mo (IV); (c) the formation of MoS2. The process could be expressed as following:
CS(NH2)2 +2H2O→2NH3 +CO2 +H2S (1).
4Na2MoO4 +15CS(NH2)2 +6H2O→4MoS2 +Na2SO4 +6NaSCN+24NH3 +9CO2 (2).

The electrochemical behavior of the different working electrodes towards the oxidation of nitrite was investigated by cyclic voltammograms (CVs), and the results are shown in Fig. 3(A). It can be seen that when adding 4.0mM nitrite in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS at a scan rate of 0.1V·s−1 (pH6.0), bare electrode (curve a) shows no significant current response while a dramatic increase of oxidation peak current signal is observed at a lower overpotential of 0.8V (curve c), which shifted negatively when compared with the peak obtained at MoS2/GCE (curve b). Therefore, it can be concluded that Au NPs/MoS2/GCE has excellent electrocatalytic properties towards the oxidation of nitrite. Fig. 3(B) displays the amperometric current-time curve of the (a) bare GCE, (b) MoS2/GCE and (c) Au NPs/MoS2/GCE in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS in the presence of 4.0mM nitrite. It was clear that when injecting 4.0mM nitrite, bare GCE (curve a) and MoS2/GCE (curve b) brought out hardly discernible current response, and a well-defined amperometric response at about 50μA was obtained by Au NPs/MoS2/GCE (curve c), indicating that Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposite own the superior catalytic effect towards nitrite. The improved electrochemical response of nitrite at Au NPs/MoS2/GCE could be contributed to the good conductivity of Au nanoparticles deposit on the 3D MoS2 nanosheets increased the active area of the electrode, thus the current intensity was enhanced.

In order to improve the performance of the sensor, we optimized the pH value of 0.1M PBS by testing the CVs of the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE towards 6.0mM nitrite. As was shown in Fig. S2, the peak currents increased when increasing the solution pHs from 4.0 to 6.0. When the solution pH was higher than 6.0, the oxidation peak current decreases sharply. The decrease in the oxidation peak current of nitrite at pH values lower than 6.0 was due to the protonation of nitrite. Besides, for the higher pH values of 7.0 and 8.0, the hydroxyl group will compete with nitrite as nucleophilic group for the interaction with the ligand, and kinetic control decreases the peak current of nitrite oxidation on the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE, so the oxidation peak current of nitrite decreased. In order to get good sensitivity for sensing nitrite, 0.1M PBS (pH6.0) has been selected for subsequent measurements.

According to the reported literature, the oxidation of nitrite is a second-order homogeneous disproportionation process in weakly acidic electrolytes [40]. The mechanism of nitrite electrooxidation can be expressed as following [41]:
(1)
H +
+
NO 2 −
↔
HNO 2
(2)
NO 2 −
↔
NO 2
+
e −
(3)
NO 2 −
+
H 2
O ↔
NO 3 −
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful tool for investigating the interfacial electron transfer characteristics of surface-modified electrodes. Generally, the semicircle diameter of Nyquist plots equaled to the electron transfer resistance (Rct). In the impedance spectra (Fig. S3), the bare GCE had a small semicircle diameter (curve a), implying low Ret to the redox probe dissolved in electrolyte solution. After MoS2 was modified onto the electrode, a remarkable increase in the semicircle diameter was observed compared with bare GCE (curve b), which was ascribed to the presence of semiconductive MoS2. In contrast, when Au NPs was immobilized onto MoS2, the Ret value was extremely reduced owing to the excellent conductivity of Au NPs that decreased the impedance of the electrode (curve c). All this proved that the Au NPs could act as an excellent electronic substrate and could contribute to much larger response surface and electron transfer passages.

The catalytic activity of Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites modified electrode by changing the concentration of nitrite as shown in Fig. 4(A). In N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (pH6.0), with the increment of nitrite concentration, the highest oxidation current responses gradually increased at 0.8V due to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites towards nitrite. Fig. 4(B) shows the calibration curves of catalytic current versus nitrite concentration. A good linear relationship is found between the nitrite concentration and catalytic current at a range from 0 to 8mM and the linear equation was ip (μA)=4.62+31.08·C (mM) (R=0.9996). Fig. 4(C) shows the corresponding CV curves of the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE containing 8mM nitrite with different scan rates. It can be found that when varying the scan rate from 0.02 to 0.2V·s−1, the oxidation peak current increased linearly with the square root of the scan rates showed a good linear relationship and the fitted linear plot can be expressed by ip (μA)=23.96(ν1/2)+697.13 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9998 (Fig. 4(D)). The result strongly demonstrated that this electrocatalytic towards nitrite on Au NPs/MoS2/GCE was a diffusion-controlled process.

Fig. 5(A) shows the amperometric response of nitrite at the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE upon the successive injection of nitrite in 0.1mol·L−1 PBS (pH6.0) with the applied potential of 0.8V. Under the optimized experimental conditions, It took <3s to achieve the steady-state current upon addition of nitrite to the stirring support electrolyte, indicating the modified electrode own fast amperometric response behavior. Fig. 5(B) shows the calibration curve of the sensor. The modified electrode exhibited a wide nitrite concentration linear range from 5.0μM to 27.8mM and the linear regression equation was ip (μA)=2.82+12.39·C (mM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9994, the sensitivity is 117.0 μAmM−1 cm−2 and the detection limit is estimated to be 1.67μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

A comparison of the Au NPs/MoS2/GCE with some typical electrochemical sensors reported before for the determination of nitrite are presented in Table 1 . The analytical parameters such as applied potential, supporting electrolyte, linear range and detection limit were compared and tabulated. It can be observed that our modified electrode fabricated in this work showed a better performance when compared to other electrodes, especially the wide linear range and the low detection limit. The superior property of the sensor may attributed by combining large supporting area of MoS2 nanospheres with more good electrocatalytic particles of Au NPs deposition, which greatly increase the electrical conductivity and catalytic activity of nanostructure and further promote the oxidation of nirite.

Some interference effects such as NaCl, NaBr, K2CO3, Al(NO3)3 and CuSO4 usually coexist with nitrite in tap water and their anion may be adsorbed or participate electro-oxidation reactions easily, so it is necessary to investigate the anti-interference capability of Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites as the nitrite sensor. Thus, the amperometric response of Au NPs/MoS2 to the successive addition of nitrite (0.05mM) and potentially interfering species including NaCl, NaBr, K2CO3, Al(NO3)3, CuSO4 (5.0mM, respectively) on modified electrode in N2-saturated 0.1M PBS (pH6.0) at a working potential of 0.8V has been studied. We can see from Fig. S4 that after successively injecting nitrite and interfering ion, only nitrite caused notable current response while other distractions were not, indicating the proposed sensor has good selectivity and anti-interference ability towards the detection of nitrite.

Under the optimum condition, the reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the modified electrode were investigated. As clarified in Fig. S5(A), compared with the response peak current of six Au NPs/MoS2/GCEs, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of this repetitive research experiment was 2.19%. Therefore, this proposed sensor had a good reproducibility. The repeatability was investigated via fabricating independently five modified electrodes to detect the 1.0×10−3 M of nitrite. After swept for 20cycles by CV, the current response of Au NPs/MoS2/GCE showed little discernible change, suggesting the good repeatability of our proposed sensor. Otherwise, the stability of the modified GCE was also studied every five days by examining the current response during storage under ambient conditions. We can find that the CV response of the electrode to the same concentration of nitrite decreased <3.69% of the initial response after one month in our work (Fig. S5(B)), demonstrating the stability of the electrode is satisfactory and it could be used for repetitive detection.

The practical application of the Au NPs/MoS2 composite modified electrode was tested to determine the concentrations of nitrite in tap water sample without any primary preparation. Tap water samples consisting of different concentrations of spiked nitrite were quantitatively analyzed using a standard addition method. The results are illustrated in Table 2 . As can be seen from the table, the noteworthy recovery changed from 93.0% to 106.6% and favorable RSD changed from 2.4% to 3.7%, indicating that the modified electrode could be effectively used for the detection of nitrite in real samples. Thus, the proposed method provides a capable alternative for the quick and easy determination of nitrite in tap water samples.

In summary, Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites were synthesized successfully via anchoring gold nanoparticles on the surface of flower-like MoS2 nanospheres. The results proved that the successful APTES-functionalized flower-like MoS2 could not only provide a unique structure and morphology for citric-stabilized gold nanoparticles anchored uniformly through electrostatic attraction but also have an active effect on nitrite sensing. Moreover, the as-prepared sensor exhibited high-performance electrochemical properties like wider linear range (0.005–27.8mM), higher sensitivity (117.0μAmM−1 cm−2) and lower detection limit (1.67μM) than other nitrite sensors previously reported. Considering the facile preparation route and excellent electrocatalytic ability of Au NPs/MoS2 nanocomposites, this original research work provides an innovative idea to bulid variform MoS2 as a supporter in nitrite sensing and it can be explored to construct many other advanced sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this project by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 21575113), Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (No. 20126101120023), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province in China (No. 2013KJXX-25), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory (15JS100, 16JS099).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2019.03.036.
